title: Curry Tripe
excerpt: Ingredients 1 sheep tripe, thoroughly cleaned 2 litres light vinegar water (45 ml vinegar per litre of water) 2 onions, roughly chopped extra water, as needed 125 ml white wine (optional) 2 – 3 bay leaves salt and white pepper to taste 45 ml sunflower oil 2 extra onions, cut into medium wedges 30 …
html:
Ingredients

  • 1 sheep tripe, thoroughly cleaned
  • 2 litres light vinegar water (45 ml vinegar per litre of water)
  • 2 onions, roughly chopped
  • extra water, as needed
  • 125 ml white wine (optional)
  • 2 – 3 bay leaves
  • salt and white pepper to taste
  • 45 ml sunflower oil
  • 2 extra onions, cut into medium wedges
  • 30 ml curry powder of your choice
  • 30 ml turmeric
  • 10 ml aniseed, coarsely crushed
  • 65 ml vinegar
  • 65 ml sugar or apricot jam (or to taste)
  • 5 – 10 ml dried red chilli flakes (or to taste)
  • 4 potatoes cut into medium blocks
  • extra vinegar and sugar or apricot jam
  • extra water and cornflour if needed
  • 65 ml finely chopped coriander leaves or parsley

Method

Cut the tripe into pieces roughly the size of the palm of your (wife's) hand.

Let the whole tripe, head and all, sit in the vinegar water for 30 minutes or so.

Drain the water, rinse the tripe well and place it in a large pot (a potjie is perfect for this recipe; just adjust the cooking time) together with the chopped onions.

Cover with cold water, add the wine (if using) and bay leaves.

Season to taste with salt and white pepper and bring to the boil.

Remove any scum that forms on top and then lower the heat.

Now cook the tripe slowly, for about 2 to 3 hours, until it just starts to become nice and tender.

Use a slotted spoon and remove any loose little bones from the pot, as well as the head and cheek bones.

Cut the skin from the head and cheeks into smaller pieces and place it back in the pot.

Heat the oil in a large pan and add the onion wedges.

Fry until glossy and then add the curry powder, turmeric and aniseed.

Fry for a minute or so until fragrant.

Then add the vinegar and sugar or jam, as well as the red chilli flakes, if using.

Stir well and spoon the curry mixture over the tripe, stir well and then stir in the potatoes.

Bring to the boil again, lower the heat and simmer with the lid on until the tripe and potatoes are lovely and tender.

If the sauce looks like too much, you can do one of two things: cook the tripe without a lid on high until the sauce has reduced enough and become nice and syrupy.

Otherwise the quick way: thicken the sauce with a paste made of cornflour and a little water.

If you think it's needed, season further to taste with extra vinegar, sugar or apricot jam and salt and pepper.

Stir in the coriander leaves or parsley and serve.

Serves 8

Serve with side dishes of your choice.

Not exactly quick, but definitely easy!
